author: @breannestahlman5953
text: Thank you, and any tricks to do the inverse: open the chain to reduce it size, then recmose it?

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: Use a punch with the point grinded off or a flat nosed punch!

author: @Barskor1
text: Thanks, Luke a washer and a plank with a hole drilled in make for a really good platform as well.

author: @DavidKenny64
text: You could also gently squeeze it in a bench vise.

author: @oscarfoxtrot99
text: Nice one Luke, takes me back and being  told "don't strangle that mallet" :)

author: @mikebond6328
text: Oh, you’re gonna catch flak for using the screwdriver as a punch.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahaha, I used to do this all the time as a kid always used a screwdriver lol! The tool we have now makes these things so easy!

replies.author: @BoB4jjjjs
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Bet you dad never had good screwdrivers  :-((

author: @keithking1985
text: can one of you try making something that will turn a petrol generator. water, air, a big fly wheel, or all of them combined somehow.. would love to see something on a genny's inside's. even a tear down video.

author: @totherarf
text: If you are doing it multiple times a Split Link is the way to go!

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Nothing like the way I did it as a kid fixing my bike

author: @wmffgeorge1589
text: I know I do that myself, but pleas get a punch, I'd even meke you one, a tourks that small 'wont' last long if you do to much of that with it.

author: @swagswap
text: I imagine a washer would avoid some of the wobble you ran into with a nut, since it would be nearly flush with the work bench. Are there other aspects of a washer that wouldn't be worth the trade off?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That's an awesome suggestion! I guess if you had quite a big washer say like m10-m12 it would work a treat but I'm unsure. Thanks mate! :)

author: @craigglewis
text: OK now a video on how to break the chain link. ;)

replies.author: @samuelthomasengardio
replies.text: All you need is a socket, a nail, and a C clamp or vice.

replies.author: @craigglewis
replies.text: @samuelthomasengardio I usually File the head of the pin and punch it out. Just wanted to see what Luke does since he did not show as the title shows. Removal ;)

author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
text: I would definitely not stick it in your tool as you suggested. Could be painful!

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Just commenting to benefit YT’s algorithm.

author: @reaneccles754
text: Old school, love it. Well done

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I don't even know where I picked this tip up from but I have done it since I was a kid lol! Thanks mate. :)

author: @JUANKERR2000
text: BUTCHER! Use a proper drift or a pin punch, not a screwdriver.
